title: Joshua: Jericho and Conquest
objective: Deepen biblical-theological understanding of joshua: jericho and conquest and its place in redemptive history.

required_passages.text: Selected key passages for this unit segment, read in full during lesson delivery.

segments.audio_script: Today we study joshua: jericho and conquest and trace God's covenant faithfulness.
Listen to the assigned passage and note covenant themes, divine actions, and human response.
We place this text in its historical setting, canonical location, and literary flow.
We analyze theological meaning, covenant development, and connections to the wider biblical story.
Key themes include God's holiness, covenant faithfulness, judgment and mercy, and redemptive purpose.

Review question: How did the walls of Jericho fall? Answer: Israel marched around the city for six days, and on the seventh day marched seven times, the priests blew the trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls collapsed.
segments.correct_answer: Israel marched for seven days, the priests blew trumpets, the people shouted, and the walls fell
acceptable_variants: they marched seven days seven times on the last day and shouted
the trumpets and shout brought the walls down
seven days of marching with priests blowing trumpets until the walls fell

segments.audio_script: Carry this lesson into prayer, worship, and careful Scripture reading as we continue the course.

vocabulary.definition: The Hebrew term for the ban or devoted destruction; in Jericho, all persons and animals were to be destroyed as an act of consecration to God — a judgment not a war tactic
The Canaanite woman of Jericho who hid the Israelite spies and was spared; she is included in Jesus' genealogy in Matthew 1 and cited in Hebrews 11 as a woman of faith
